title: VII
html:
VII

Arabella was preparing breakfast in the downstairs back room of this small, recently hired tenement of her father‚Äôs. She put her head into the little pork-shop in front, and told Mr.¬ÝDonn it was ready. Donn, endeavouring to look like a master pork-butcher, in a greasy blue blouse, and with a strap round his waist from which a steel dangled, came in promptly.

‚ÄúYou must mind the shop this morning,‚Äù he said casually. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve to go and get some inwards and half a pig from Lumsdon, and to call elsewhere. If you live here you must put your shoulder to the wheel, at least till I get the business started!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, for today I can‚Äôt say.‚Äù She looked deedily into his face. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve got a prize upstairs.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh?Ôªø‚ÄîWhat‚Äôs that?‚Äù

‚ÄúA husbandÔªø‚Äîalmost.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo!‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. It‚Äôs Jude. He‚Äôs come back to me.‚Äù

‚ÄúYour old original one? Well, I‚Äôm damned!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, I always did like him, that I will say.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut how does he come to be up there?‚Äù said Donn, humour-struck, and nodding to the ceiling.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt ask inconvenient questions, father. What we‚Äôve to do is to keep him here till he and I areÔªø‚Äîas we were.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow was that?‚Äù

‚ÄúMarried.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ Well it is the rummest thing I ever heard ofÔªø‚Äîmarrying an old husband again, and so much new blood in the world! He‚Äôs no catch, to my thinking. I‚Äôd have had a new one while I was about it.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt isn‚Äôt rum for a woman to want her old husband back for respectability, though for a man to want his old wife backÔªø‚Äîwell, perhaps it is funny, rather!‚Äù And Arabella was suddenly seized with a fit of loud laughter, in which her father joined more moderately.

‚ÄúBe civil to him, and I‚Äôll do the rest,‚Äù she said when she had recovered seriousness. ‚ÄúHe told me this morning that his head ached fit to burst, and he hardly seemed to know where he was. And no wonder, considering how he mixed his drink last night. We must keep him jolly and cheerful here for a day or two, and not let him go back to his lodging. Whatever you advance I‚Äôll pay back to you again. But I must go up and see how he is now, poor deary.‚Äù

Arabella ascended the stairs, softly opened the door of the first bedroom, and peeped in. Finding that her shorn Samson was asleep she entered to the bedside and stood regarding him. The fevered flush on his face from the debauch of the previous evening lessened the fragility of his ordinary appearance, and his long lashes, dark brows, and curly black hair and beard against the white pillow, completed the physiognomy of one whom Arabella, as a woman of rank passions, still felt it worth while to recapture, highly important to recapture as a woman straitened both in means and in reputation. Her ardent gaze seemed to affect him; his quick breathing became suspended, and he opened his eyes.

‚ÄúHow are you now, dear?‚Äù said she. ‚ÄúIt is IÔªø‚ÄîArabella.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh!Ôªø‚ÄîwhereÔªø‚ÄîO yes, I remember! You gave me shelter.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ I am strandedÔªø‚ÄîillÔªø‚ÄîdemoralizedÔªø‚Äîdamn bad! That‚Äôs what I am!‚Äù

‚ÄúThen do stay there. There‚Äôs nobody in the house but father and me, and you can rest till you are thoroughly well. I‚Äôll tell them at the stone-works that you are knocked up.‚Äù

‚ÄúI wonder what they are thinking at the lodgings!‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôll go round and explain. Perhaps you had better let me pay up, or they‚Äôll think we‚Äôve run away?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. You‚Äôll find enough money in my pocket there.‚Äù

Quite indifferent, and shutting his eyes because he could not bear the daylight in his throbbing eyeballs, Jude seemed to doze again. Arabella took his purse, softly left the room, and putting on her outdoor things went off to the lodgings she and he had quitted the evening before.

Scarcely half-an-hour had elapsed ere she reappeared round the corner, walking beside a lad wheeling a truck on which were piled all Jude‚Äôs household possessions, and also the few of Arabella‚Äôs things which she had taken to the lodging for her short sojourn there. Jude was in such physical pain from his unfortunate breakdown of the previous night, and in such mental pain from the loss of Sue and from having yielded in his half-somnolent state to Arabella, that when he saw his few chattels unpacked and standing before his eyes in this strange bedroom, intermixed with woman‚Äôs apparel, he scarcely considered how they had come there, or what their coming signalized.

‚ÄúNow,‚Äù said Arabella to her father downstairs, ‚Äúwe must keep plenty of good liquor going in the house these next few days. I know his nature, and if he once gets into that fearfully low state that he does get into sometimes, he‚Äôll never do the honourable thing by me in this world, and I shall be left in the lurch. He must be kept cheerful. He has a little money in the savings-bank, and he has given me his purse to pay for anything necessary. Well, that will be the license; for I must have that ready at hand, to catch him the moment he‚Äôs in the humour. You must pay for the liquor. A few friends, and a quiet convivial party would be the thing, if we could get it up. It would advertise the shop, and help me too.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat can be got up easy enough by anybody who‚Äôll afford victuals and drink.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ Well yesÔªø‚Äîit would advertise the shopÔªø‚Äîthat‚Äôs true.‚Äù

Three days later, when Jude had recovered somewhat from the fearful throbbing of his eyes and brain, but was still considerably confused in his mind by what had been supplied to him by Arabella during the intervalÔªø‚Äîto keep him jolly, as she expressed itÔªø‚Äîthe quiet convivial gathering suggested by her, to wind Jude up to the striking point, took place.

Donn had only just opened his miserable little pork and sausage shop, which had as yet scarce any customers; nevertheless that party advertised it well, and the Donns acquired a real notoriety among a certain class in Christminster who knew not the colleges, nor their works, nor their ways. Jude was asked if he could suggest any guest in addition to those named by Arabella and her father, and in a saturnine humour of perfect recklessness mentioned Uncle Joe, and Stagg, and the decayed auctioneer, and others whom he remembered as having been frequenters of the well-known tavern during his bout therein years before. He also suggested Freckles and Bower o‚Äô Bliss. Arabella took him at his word so far as the men went, but drew the line at the ladies.

Another man they knew, Tinker Taylor, though he lived in the same street, was not invited; but as he went homeward from a late job on the evening of the party, he had occasion to call at the shop for trotters. There were none in, but he was promised some the next morning. While making his inquiry Taylor glanced into the back room, and saw the guests sitting round, card-playing, and drinking, and otherwise enjoying themselves at Donn‚Äôs expense. He went home to bed, and on his way out next morning wondered how the party went off. He thought it hardly worth while to call at the shop for his provisions at that hour, Donn and his daughter being probably not up, if they caroused late the night before. However, he found in passing that the door was open, and he could hear voices within, though the shutters of the meat-stall were not down. He went and tapped at the sitting-room door, and opened it.

‚ÄúWellÔªø‚Äîto be sure!‚Äù he said, astonished.

Hosts and guests were sitting card-playing, smoking, and talking, precisely as he had left them eleven hours earlier; the gas was burning and the curtains drawn, though it had been broad daylight for two hours out of doors.

‚ÄúYes!‚Äù cried Arabella, laughing. ‚ÄúHere we are, just the same. We ought to be ashamed of ourselves, oughtn‚Äôt we! But it is a sort of housewarming, you see; and our friends are in no hurry. Come in, Mr.¬ÝTaylor, and sit down.‚Äù

The tinker, or rather reduced ironmonger, was nothing loth, and entered and took a seat. ‚ÄúI shall lose a quarter, but never mind,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúWell, really, I could hardly believe my eyes when I looked in! It seemed as if I was flung back again into last night, all of a sudden.‚Äù

‚ÄúSo you are. Pour out for Mr.¬ÝTaylor.‚Äù

He now perceived that she was sitting beside Jude, her arm being round his waist. Jude, like the rest of the company, bore on his face the signs of how deeply he had been indulging.

‚ÄúWell, we‚Äôve been waiting for certain legal hours to arrive, to tell the truth,‚Äù she continued bashfully, and making her spirituous crimson look as much like a maiden blush as possible. ‚ÄúJude and I have decided to make up matters between us by tying the knot again, as we find we can‚Äôt do without one another after all. So, as a bright notion, we agreed to sit on till it was late enough, and go and do it offhand.‚Äù

Jude seemed to pay no great heed to what she was announcing, or indeed to anything whatever. The entrance of Taylor infused fresh spirit into the company, and they remained sitting, till Arabella whispered to her father: ‚ÄúNow we may as well go.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut the parson don‚Äôt know?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, I told him last night that we might come between eight and nine, as there were reasons of decency for doing it as early and quiet as possible; on account of it being our second marriage, which might make people curious to look on if they knew. He highly approved.‚Äù

‚ÄúO very well: I‚Äôm ready,‚Äù said her father, getting up and shaking himself.

‚ÄúNow, old darling,‚Äù she said to Jude. ‚ÄúCome along, as you promised.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhen did I promise anything?‚Äù asked he, whom she had made so tipsy by her special knowledge of that line of business as almost to have made him sober againÔªø‚Äîor to seem so to those who did not know him.

‚ÄúWhy!‚Äù said Arabella, affecting dismay. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve promised to marry me several times as we‚Äôve sat here tonight. These gentlemen have heard you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt remember it,‚Äù said Jude doggedly. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs only one womanÔªø‚Äîbut I won‚Äôt mention her in this Capharnaum!‚Äù

Arabella looked towards her father. ‚ÄúNow, Mr.¬ÝFawley, be honourable,‚Äù said Donn. ‚ÄúYou and my daughter have been living here together these three or four days, quite on the understanding that you were going to marry her. Of course I shouldn‚Äôt have had such goings on in my house if I hadn‚Äôt understood that. As a point of honour you must do it now.‚Äù

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt say anything against my honour!‚Äù enjoined Jude hotly, standing up. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd marry the WÔªø‚ÄîÔªø‚Äî of Babylon rather than do anything dishonourable! No reflection on you, my dear. It is a mere rhetorical figureÔªø‚Äîwhat they call in the books, hyperbole.‚Äù

‚ÄúKeep your figures for your debts to friends who shelter you,‚Äù said Donn.

‚ÄúIf I am bound in honour to marry herÔªø‚Äîas I suppose I amÔªø‚Äîthough how I came to be here with her I know no more than a dead manÔªø‚Äîmarry her I will, so help me God! I have never behaved dishonourably to a woman or to any living thing. I am not a man who wants to save himself at the expense of the weaker among us!‚Äù

‚ÄúThereÔªø‚Äînever mind him, deary,‚Äù said she, putting her cheek against Jude‚Äôs. ‚ÄúCome up and wash your face, and just put yourself tidy, and off we‚Äôll go. Make it up with father.‚Äù

They shook hands. Jude went upstairs with her, and soon came down looking tidy and calm. Arabella, too, had hastily arranged herself, and accompanied by Donn away they went.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt go,‚Äù she said to the guests at parting. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve told the little maid to get the breakfast while we are gone; and when we come back we‚Äôll all have some. A good strong cup of tea will set everybody right for going home.‚Äù

When Arabella, Jude and Donn had disappeared on their matrimonial errand the assembled guests yawned themselves wider awake, and discussed the situation with great interest. Tinker Taylor, being the most sober, reasoned the most lucidly.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt wish to speak against friends,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúBut it do seem a rare curiosity for a couple to marry over again! If they couldn‚Äôt get on the first time when their minds were limp, they won‚Äôt the second, by my reckoning.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you think he‚Äôll do it?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôs been put upon his honour by the woman, so he med.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôd hardly do it straight off like this. He‚Äôs got no license nor anything.‚Äù

‚ÄúShe‚Äôs got that, bless you. Didn‚Äôt you hear her say so to her father?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell,‚Äù said Tinker Taylor, re-lighting his pipe at the gas-jet. ‚ÄúTake her all together, limb by limb, she‚Äôs not such a bad-looking pieceÔªø‚Äîparticular by candlelight. To be sure, halfpence that have been in circulation can‚Äôt be expected to look like new ones from the Mint. But for a woman that‚Äôs been knocking about the four hemispheres for some time, she‚Äôs passable enough. A little bit thick in the flitch perhaps: but I like a woman that a puff o‚Äô wind won‚Äôt blow down.‚Äù

Their eyes followed the movements of the little girl as she spread the breakfast-cloth on the table they had been using, without wiping up the slops of the liquor. The curtains were undrawn, and the expression of the house made to look like morning. Some of the guests, however, fell asleep in their chairs. One or two went to the door, and gazed along the street more than once. Tinker Taylor was the chief of these, and after a time he came in with a leer on his face.

‚ÄúBy Gad, they are coming! I think the deed‚Äôs done!‚Äù

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù said Uncle Joe, following him in. ‚ÄúTake my word, he turned rusty at the last minute. They are walking in a very onusual way; and that‚Äôs the meaning of it!‚Äù

They waited in silence till the wedding party could be heard entering the house. First into the room came Arabella boisterously; and her face was enough to show that her strategy had succeeded.

‚ÄúMrs.¬ÝFawley, I presume?‚Äù said Tinker Taylor with mock courtesy.

‚ÄúCertainly. Mrs.¬ÝFawley again,‚Äù replied Arabella blandly, pulling off her glove and holding out her left hand. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs the padlock, see.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ Well, he was a very, nice, gentlemanly man indeed. I mean the clergyman. He said to me as gentle as a babe when all was done: ‚ÄòMrs.¬ÝFawley, I congratulate you heartily,‚Äô he says. ‚ÄòFor having heard your history, and that of your husband, I think you have both done the right and proper thing. And for your past errors as a wife, and his as a husband, I think you ought now to be forgiven by the world, as you have forgiven each other,‚Äô says he. Yes: he was a very, nice, gentlemanly man. ‚ÄòThe Church don‚Äôt recognize divorce in her dogma, strictly speaking,‚Äô he says: ‚Äòand bear in mind the words of the Service in your goings out and your comings in: What God hath joined together let no man put asunder.‚Äô Yes: he was a very nice, gentlemanly man.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ But, Jude, my dear, you were enough to make a cat laugh! You walked that straight, and held yourself that steady, that one would have thought you were going ‚Äôprentice to a judge; though I knew you were seeing double all the time, from the way you fumbled with my finger.‚Äù

‚ÄúI said I‚Äôd do anything toÔªø‚Äîsave a woman‚Äôs honour,‚Äù muttered Jude. ‚ÄúAnd I‚Äôve done it!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell now, old deary, come along and have some breakfast.‚Äù

‚ÄúI wantÔªø‚ÄîsomeÔªø‚Äîmore whisky,‚Äù said Jude stolidly.

‚ÄúNonsense, dear. Not now! There‚Äôs no more left. The tea will take the muddle out of our heads, and we shall be as fresh as larks.‚Äù

‚ÄúAll right. I‚ÄôveÔªø‚Äîmarried you. She said I ought to marry you again, and I have straightway. It is true religion! HaÔªø‚ÄîhaÔªø‚Äîha!‚Äù
